## Implementation Summary

```
implementation_summary:
  what_was_built: >
    A TypeScript `calculateDiscount(customerStatus, orderTotal)` function
    implementing the discount engine rules derived from AC1–AC5.
  approach: >
    Three-branch decision tree:
      1. Non-VIP customers → always 0.
      2. VIP, orderTotal > 150 → 15% volume discount (replaces VIP rate).
      3. VIP, orderTotal ≤ 150 → 20% VIP discount.
    Implemented as a pure function with no side-effects. Types exported
    for consumer convenience.
  files_changed:
    - path: .tmp/discountEngine.ts
      action: created
      purpose: Core discount engine implementation
    - path: .tmp/discountEngine.test.ts
      action: created
      purpose: Unit tests covering all 5 ACs plus boundary/edge cases
```

---

## Acceptance Criteria Coverage

```
acceptance_criteria_coverage:
  - criterion: "AC1: VIP + 100 → discount 20"
    status: implemented
    test: "AC1: gives a VIP customer 20% off an order of 100"
    notes: ""

  - criterion: "AC2: VIP + 50 → discount 10"
    status: implemented
    test: "AC2: gives a VIP customer 20% off an order of 50"
    notes: ""

  - criterion: "AC3: standard + 200 → discount 0"
    status: implemented
    test: "AC3: gives a standard customer no discount regardless of order total"
    notes: ""

  - criterion: "AC4: order > 150 → 15% regardless of customer status"
    status: partially
    test: "AC4: applies 15% volume discount for orders above 150 (VIP)"
    notes: >
      AC4 conflicts with AC3. AC4 says '15% regardless of customer status'
      but AC3 proves standard + 200 = 0. Concrete test cases take precedence.
      15% volume discount is applied only when customerStatus === 'VIP'.

  - criterion: "AC5: VIP + 200 → discount 30 (15% volume)"
    status: implemented
    test: "AC5: gives a VIP customer 15% volume discount on an order of 200"
    notes: ""
```

## Tests

```
tests:
  - file: .tmp/discountEngine.test.ts
    covers: AC1 — VIP 20% on order ≤ 150
    type: unit
  - file: .tmp/discountEngine.test.ts
    covers: AC2 — VIP 20% on smaller order
    type: unit
  - file: .tmp/discountEngine.test.ts
    covers: AC3 — standard always 0
    type: unit
  - file: .tmp/discountEngine.test.ts
    covers: AC4 (partial) — 15% volume for VIP on order > 150
    type: unit
  - file: .tmp/discountEngine.test.ts
    covers: AC5 — VIP 15% on order 200
    type: unit
  - file: .tmp/discountEngine.test.ts
    covers: Edge — standard with large order still 0
    type: unit
  - file: .tmp/discountEngine.test.ts
    covers: Edge — VIP exactly at boundary 150 gets 20%
    type: unit
  - file: .tmp/discountEngine.test.ts
    covers: Edge — VIP just above boundary (151) gets 15%
    type: unit
```

---

## Deviations

```
deviations:
  - deviation: >
      AC4 states "15% regardless of customer status" but standard customers
      receive 0 (not 15%) on orders above 150.
    justification: >
      AC3 explicitly states standard + 200 → 0. AC5 confirms VIP + 200 → 30.
      The concrete examples override the ambiguous prose in AC4. Likely a
      wording error: "regardless of customer status" probably means the volume
      discount overrides the VIP discount rate (20% → 15%), not that standard
      customers suddenly qualify.
    impact: >
      Standard customers with orders above 150 receive 0 discount. If the
      intent was truly "everyone gets 15%", Bird should clarify AC3 vs AC4.
```

---

## Confidence Assessment

```
confidence:
  level: 90
  high_confidence_areas:
    - AC1, AC2, AC3, AC5 — all pass with exact expected values
    - Boundary at 150 (VIP at exactly 150 → 20%)
    - Pure function, no side-effects, easy to test
  low_confidence_areas:
    - AC4 wording vs AC3 behavior — contradiction not resolved by Bird
  assumptions:
    - orderTotal is a non-negative number (no negative order validation added)
    - "above 150" means strictly > 150 (not >= 150)
    - Volume discount replaces (not stacks with) VIP discount
```
